purposes.description: Convert sequential Python loops and batch operations into parallel implementations using multiprocessing, concurrent.futures, or asyncio, choosing the right API based on workload type (CPU-bound vs I/O-bound).

primitives.evidence: Skill requires generating Python code using standard library modules: multiprocessing.Pool, concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor, os.cpu_count. All examples use stdlib, no third-party libraries.
Agent must identify the workload type (CPU-bound vs I/O-bound) from user's sequential code to select the right parallelization strategy from the decision framework table.
Agent must plan the transformation: identify the loop body, extract it into a worker function, choose pool.map vs apply_async vs executor.map, and handle result collection order.
Multiple constraints: avoid shared mutable state, use context managers for resource cleanup, use __name__ guard on Windows/macOS, preserve output ordering when required, handle errors per-item.

purposes.description: Optimize parallel code by selecting worker count, chunk_size, and choosing between process vs thread pools based on workload characteristics.

primitives.evidence: Generating Python code with os.cpu_count(), chunksize parameter tuning, and Pool(processes=N) configuration.
Computing chunk_size = max(1, len(items) // (n_workers * 4)) and reasoning about IPC overhead tradeoffs.
